meta.title: Entra ID – Microsoft Entra Domain Services
meta.subject: Microsoft Entra ID

questions.question_text: What is Microsoft Entra Domain Services?

a.text: A tool for syncing on-premises Active Directory to Entra ID
a.explanation: Incorrect. That describes Entra Connect. Entra Domain Services is a managed domain service, not a sync tool.

b.text: A managed domain service providing Kerberos, NTLM, LDAP, and Group Policy without deploying domain controllers
b.explanation: Correct. Entra Domain Services is a PaaS offering that provides traditional AD DS services (Kerberos, NTLM, LDAP, domain join, Group Policy) in Azure without requiring customers to deploy or manage domain controllers.

c.text: A backup service for on-premises Active Directory
c.explanation: Incorrect. Entra Domain Services is not an AD backup solution.

d.text: An upgrade path from Windows Server AD DS to Entra ID
d.explanation: Incorrect. Entra Domain Services is a parallel managed service, not a migration or upgrade path.

questions.question_text: Which legacy authentication protocols does Microsoft Entra Domain Services support? Select all that apply.

a.text: Kerberos
a.explanation: Correct. Entra Domain Services supports Kerberos authentication, which is required by many legacy Windows applications.

b.text: NTLM
b.explanation: Correct. Entra Domain Services supports NTLM authentication for legacy apps that cannot use Kerberos.

c.text: LDAP
c.explanation: Correct. Entra Domain Services supports LDAP for directory queries — allowing legacy apps that use LDAP to work without modification.

d.text: SAML 2.0
d.explanation: Incorrect. SAML 2.0 is a modern web SSO protocol used by Entra ID itself — it is not a legacy protocol supported by Entra Domain Services for on-premises app compatibility.

questions.question_text: Which Entra ID license is required to use Microsoft Entra Domain Services?

a.text: Entra ID Free
a.explanation: Incorrect. Entra Domain Services requires a paid tier — it is not available in the Free tier.

b.text: Entra ID P1 or P2
b.explanation: Correct. Microsoft Entra Domain Services requires Entra ID P1 or P2. The service runs as part of the P1/P2 tier.

c.text: Microsoft 365 Business Basic only
c.explanation: Incorrect. Microsoft 365 Business Basic includes Entra ID Free features — not Entra Domain Services.

d.text: Azure subscription — no Entra license needed beyond the subscription
d.explanation: Incorrect. An Azure subscription alone does not grant access to Entra Domain Services. An Entra ID P1 or P2 license is explicitly required.

questions.question_text: A developer needs to lift-and-shift a legacy .NET application that uses LDAP authentication to Azure VMs. The organisation does not want to manage domain controllers. What is the best solution?

a.text: Deploy AD DS domain controllers as Azure IaaS VMs
a.explanation: Incorrect. This would work technically, but requires the organisation to manage domain controllers — which the question explicitly says they want to avoid.

b.text: Configure a site-to-site VPN and authenticate through on-premises AD
b.explanation: Incorrect. This works but creates a dependency on on-premises infrastructure and VPN availability — not ideal for cloud workloads.

c.text: Enable Microsoft Entra Domain Services in Azure
c.explanation: Correct. Entra Domain Services provides managed LDAP, Kerberos, and NTLM services in Azure without deploying or managing domain controllers. It is the ideal solution for lifting legacy LDAP-dependent apps to Azure.

d.text: Rewrite the application to use Entra ID REST API authentication
d.explanation: Incorrect. Rewriting the application is a valid long-term approach but defeats the purpose of a lift-and-shift migration.

questions.question_text: What is a key structural limitation of the Organizational Unit (OU) support in Entra Domain Services?

a.text: OUs are not supported at all
a.explanation: Incorrect. Entra Domain Services does support OUs, but with significant limitations.

b.text: OUs are supported but the structure is flat — nested OUs are not supported
b.explanation: Correct. Entra Domain Services supports OUs but only in a flat structure. You cannot create nested OUs like you would in a traditional AD DS deployment.

c.text: OUs can only contain computer objects, not users
c.explanation: Incorrect. This is not a stated limitation. The key OU limitation is the flat structure.

d.text: You can create nested OUs but only up to 2 levels deep
d.explanation: Incorrect. The limitation is that nested OUs are not currently supported at all in Entra Domain Services.

questions.question_text: Can schema extensions be added to a Microsoft Entra Domain Services managed domain?

a.text: Yes — schema extensions are supported and work the same as in AD DS
a.explanation: Incorrect. Schema extension is explicitly listed as a limitation of Entra Domain Services.

b.text: No — the schema cannot be extended in Entra Domain Services
b.explanation: Correct. Extending the Entra Domain Services schema is not supported. This is one of the key limitations compared to deploying your own AD DS where full schema extensions are possible.

c.text: Yes — but only with an Entra ID P2 license
c.explanation: Incorrect. Schema extension is not a matter of licensing — it is an architectural limitation of the managed service.

d.text: Yes — you can submit schema extension requests to Microsoft
d.explanation: Incorrect. Customers cannot extend the Entra Domain Services schema, even via a request.

questions.question_text: How is Entra Domain Services billed?

a.text: A flat monthly fee per tenant
a.explanation: Incorrect. Entra Domain Services is billed per hour, not a flat monthly fee.

b.text: Per user per month
b.explanation: Incorrect. Entra DS is billed based on the size of the directory, not per user.

c.text: Per hour, based on the size of the directory
c.explanation: Correct. Entra Domain Services charges per hour based on the size of the managed domain (number of objects/users). You enable it via the Azure portal.

d.text: Free — included with any Azure subscription
d.explanation: Incorrect. Entra Domain Services has a separate per-hour charge and also requires Entra ID P1 licensing.

questions.question_text: What are the Group Policy limitations in Microsoft Entra Domain Services?

a.text: Group Policy is not supported at all in Entra Domain Services
a.explanation: Incorrect. Entra Domain Services does support Group Policy — but only built-in GPOs, with limitations.

b.text: Only built-in GPOs are available; you cannot use WMI filters or security-group filtering on GPOs
b.explanation: Correct. Entra Domain Services provides built-in GPOs for computer and user accounts. However, WMI filter-based targeting and security-group filtering of GPOs are not supported. You also cannot target OUs with custom nested structure.

c.text: Custom GPOs can be created but only applied at the domain level
c.explanation: Incorrect. You can create custom GPOs and link them to the flat OU structure, but the limitation is specifically around WMI filters and security-group filtering.

d.text: Group Policy works identically to on-premises AD DS
d.explanation: Incorrect. There are several GPO limitations in Entra Domain Services compared to full AD DS.

questions.question_text: A company has no on-premises Active Directory at all. Can they use Microsoft Entra Domain Services?

a.text: No — Entra Domain Services requires an on-premises AD DS deployment to sync from
a.explanation: Incorrect. Entra Domain Services can operate as a cloud-only managed domain without any on-premises AD DS.

b.text: Yes — Entra Domain Services can be used as a cloud-only service
b.explanation: Correct. Entra Domain Services works in a cloud-only scenario. An organisation without on-premises AD can still enable the managed domain, create cloud users in Entra ID, and have those users use domain services (Kerberos, NTLM, LDAP) from the managed domain.

c.text: No — Entra Domain Services requires Entra Connect to be installed on-premises
c.explanation: Incorrect. Entra Connect is only needed if you want to sync on-premises identities. For a cloud-only scenario, Entra Domain Services works independently.

d.text: Yes — but only for read-only LDAP queries
d.explanation: Incorrect. Cloud-only Entra Domain Services is not limited to read-only LDAP. Full domain services are available.

questions.question_text: Which two groups are NOT required when using Entra Domain Services, unlike a traditional AD DS deployment?

a.text: Domain Admins and Enterprise Admins
a.explanation: Correct. Because Microsoft manages the Entra Domain Services infrastructure, you do not need Domain Admins or Enterprise Admins groups. Microsoft handles all domain controller management, patching, and replication.

b.text: Security groups and Distribution groups
b.explanation: Incorrect. Security and distribution groups are standard Entra ID objects that you still create and manage. They are not eliminated by Entra Domain Services.

c.text: Schema Admins and Guest accounts
c.explanation: Incorrect. While Schema Admins are irrelevant (schema cannot be extended), this is not the intended answer. The key groups mentioned in the Microsoft Learn content are Domain Admins and Enterprise Admins.

d.text: Global Administrators and Helpdesk Administrators
d.explanation: Incorrect. These are Entra ID directory roles still used in your organisation — they are not related to Entra Domain Services managed domain roles.

questions.question_text: What happens to on-premises users synced via Entra Connect when Entra Domain Services is enabled?

a.text: Synced users are automatically registered as new cloud-only identities
a.explanation: Incorrect. Synced users retain their identity source from on-premises AD — they are not converted to cloud-only identities.

b.text: Synced users can use the same on-premises credentials in Entra Domain Services
b.explanation: Correct. When Entra Connect syncs on-premises users to Entra ID, and Entra Domain Services is enabled, users can use their organisational credentials (synced via PHS) in both the on-premises AD DS and the Entra Domain Services managed domain.

c.text: Synced users must create a new separate password for Entra Domain Services
c.explanation: Incorrect. With PHS, the password hash synced to Entra ID is also used by Entra Domain Services, so no separate password is needed.

d.text: Synced users cannot access Entra Domain Services — only cloud-only users can
d.explanation: Incorrect. Both cloud-only and synced users can use Entra Domain Services.

questions.question_text: A company has SQL Server and SharePoint Server deployed on Azure VMs and wants them to use domain-joined authentication without managing domain controllers. What should they configure?

a.text: Deploy AD DS domain controllers in Azure and manage them manually
a.explanation: Incorrect. This works but requires managing domain controllers — contradicting the requirement.

b.text: Enable Entra Domain Services and join the VMs to the managed domain
b.explanation: Correct. Entra Domain Services provides domain join, Kerberos, and domain authentication for Azure VMs — SQL Server and SharePoint VMs can be joined to the managed domain without any domain controllers to maintain.

c.text: Use Entra ID application registrations for SQL Server and SharePoint auth
c.explanation: Incorrect. Application registrations are for web/API app authentication — not for traditional Windows domain join scenarios.

d.text: Configure a site-to-site VPN and use on-premises domain controllers
d.explanation: Incorrect. While possible, this introduces dependency on on-premises infrastructure and VPN, which Entra Domain Services is specifically designed to avoid.

questions.question_text: What type of computer object is supported in Entra Domain Services?

a.text: Full computer objects with all AD DS attributes including logon scripts and software installation
a.explanation: Incorrect. Entra Domain Services only supports the base computer AD object — not the full set of attributes available in on-premises AD DS.

b.text: Only the base computer Active Directory object — extended attributes are not supported
b.explanation: Correct. Entra Domain Services supports only the base computer object. This means you cannot extend it with custom attributes or use all the AD DS computer management features.

c.text: No computer objects — only user objects are supported
c.explanation: Incorrect. Computer objects are supported — but only the base object, without schema extensions.

d.text: Computer objects with full Group Policy enforcement including software deployment
d.explanation: Incorrect. Software deployment via GPO and extended GPO targeting (WMI filters) are not supported in Entra Domain Services.

questions.question_text: Which approach avoids VPN dependency AND eliminates the need to manage domain controllers for cloud-hosted legacy apps?

a.text: Deploy replica domain controllers as Azure VMs
a.explanation: Incorrect. Replica DCs in Azure avoid VPN dependency for authentication, but still require customer management of domain controllers.

b.text: Site-to-site VPN with on-premises domain controllers
b.explanation: Incorrect. A VPN creates on-premises dependency and requires VPN infrastructure management.

c.text: Microsoft Entra Domain Services
c.explanation: Correct. Entra Domain Services provides domain services directly in Azure with no VPN required and no domain controllers to manage. Microsoft provides all the infrastructure.

d.text: Azure ExpressRoute with on-premises domain controllers
d.explanation: Incorrect. ExpressRoute provides a private connection but still depends on on-premises domain controllers and their management.

questions.question_text: Who manages Active Directory replication in a Microsoft Entra Domain Services deployment?

a.text: The customer's AD administrator
a.explanation: Incorrect. Entra Domain Services is a managed service — customers do not manage AD replication.

b.text: Microsoft manages replication as part of the service
b.explanation: Correct. One of the key benefits of Entra Domain Services is that Microsoft manages domain controller deployment, patching, and AD replication. Customers have no operational responsibility for the underlying infrastructure.

c.text: Entra Connect manages replication as part of the sync process
c.explanation: Incorrect. Entra Connect handles identity synchronisation from on-premises AD to Entra ID — it does not manage Entra Domain Services replication.

d.text: There is no replication — Entra Domain Services is single-instance only
d.explanation: Incorrect. Entra Domain Services does replicate data internally for resilience — but this is fully managed by Microsoft.

questions.question_text: How do you provision a Microsoft Entra Domain Services managed domain?

a.text: Install a domain controller agent on an Azure VM and register it
a.explanation: Incorrect. Entra Domain Services is fully managed — there are no agents or VMs to install for the domain service itself.

b.text: Through the Azure portal — enable Entra Domain Services for a virtual network
b.explanation: Correct. Entra Domain Services is enabled through the Azure portal. You configure it for a virtual network in your Azure subscription — Microsoft provisions and manages the underlying domain controller infrastructure.

c.text: Run a PowerShell script on an on-premises domain controller to extend it to Azure
c.explanation: Incorrect. Entra Domain Services is a cloud-native managed service — not an extension of on-premises domain controllers.

d.text: Open a Microsoft support ticket to request domain services provisioning
d.explanation: Incorrect. Entra Domain Services is self-service — customers enable it via the Azure portal without opening support tickets.

questions.question_text: A company wants to migrate their on-premises SharePoint farm to Azure VMs. SharePoint uses Windows Authentication (Kerberos/NTLM) against AD. The company wants NO on-premises dependencies after migration. What should they use?

a.text: Entra ID with application registration and OAuth
a.explanation: Incorrect. Rewriting SharePoint to use OAuth/Entra ID native authentication is a major code change, not a lift-and-shift migration.

b.text: Entra Domain Services for domain join and Kerberos/NTLM, with VMs joined to the managed domain
b.explanation: Correct. Entra Domain Services provides Kerberos and NTLM authentication in Azure. SharePoint VMs can be joined to the managed domain, fulfilling the Windows Authentication requirement with no on-premises dependency.

c.text: Azure Active Directory B2C for end-user authentication
c.explanation: Incorrect. B2C is for customer-facing app authentication — not for SharePoint server farm internal Windows authentication.

d.text: Entra Connect with ADFS in Azure
d.explanation: Incorrect. Deploying ADFS in Azure still requires managing VM-based infrastructure — not the simplest solution and not necessary for SharePoint's Kerberos/NTLM needs.

questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes the difference between deploying AD DS on Azure VMs versus using Entra Domain Services?

a.text: Both require the customer to manage domain controllers and AD replication
a.explanation: Incorrect. AD DS on VMs requires customer management; Entra Domain Services has Microsoft manage the infrastructure.

b.text: AD DS on VMs: customer-managed IaaS; Entra Domain Services: Microsoft-managed PaaS with no DC management needed
b.explanation: Correct. AD DS on Azure VMs is an IaaS deployment — the customer manages the OS, DC roles, patching, and replication. Entra Domain Services is a PaaS offering where Microsoft manages everything, and customers just configure the service.

c.text: Entra Domain Services supports more AD DS features than running AD DS on Azure VMs
c.explanation: Incorrect. AD DS on Azure VMs gives you the full feature set of a traditional AD DS deployment, including schema extensions and nested OUs. Entra Domain Services has more limitations.

d.text: Neither option allows domain-joining Azure VMs
d.explanation: Incorrect. Both options support domain-joining Azure VMs.

questions.question_text: An application team reports that their app requires LDAP Secure (LDAPS) to connect to the domain. Is this supported by Entra Domain Services?

a.text: No — Entra Domain Services only supports plain LDAP over port 389
a.explanation: Incorrect. Entra Domain Services supports Secure LDAP (LDAPS) over port 636, which can be explicitly enabled in the configuration.

b.text: Yes — Secure LDAP (LDAPS) can be enabled for Entra Domain Services
b.explanation: Correct. Entra Domain Services supports LDAPS (Secure LDAP) over port 636. It must be explicitly enabled and requires a certificate. This allows encrypted LDAP connections from applications.

c.text: LDAPS is automatically enabled and cannot be disabled
c.explanation: Incorrect. LDAPS must be explicitly configured — it is not enabled by default.

d.text: LDAPS is only available for on-premises AD DS, not for Entra Domain Services
d.explanation: Incorrect. Entra Domain Services explicitly supports LDAPS as a configurable option.

questions.question_text: When using Entra Domain Services with a hybrid environment, users log in to their domain-joined Azure VMs. Which password is used?

a.text: A separate password set specifically for Entra Domain Services
a.explanation: Incorrect. Users do not need a separate password for Entra Domain Services.

b.text: Their on-premises AD password, synced to Entra ID via PHS and used by Entra Domain Services
b.explanation: Correct. In a hybrid scenario with Password Hash Sync, users use their on-premises AD credentials. The password hash is synced to Entra ID, which Entra Domain Services uses for authentication. Users have one password across on-premises AD, Entra ID, and Entra Domain Services.

c.text: Their Microsoft Account (personal) password
c.explanation: Incorrect. Microsoft personal accounts are not used for Entra Domain Services managed domain authentication in enterprise scenarios.

d.text: They must use a PIN set up through Windows Hello
d.explanation: Incorrect. Windows Hello is a modern authentication method for Entra-joined devices — it is not a prerequisite for Entra Domain Services domain authentication.
